title: After Fairhair: succession chaos
content: After Harald Fairhair's death, multiple sons and claimants competed for power. Rivalries, exile, and killings made stable kingship difficult. Norway remained politically fragmented despite the earlier unification ideal.
next: How did Christianity become tied to stronger kingship?

title: Olaf Tryggvason and forced conversion
content: Olaf Tryggvason pursued rapid Christianization with both persuasion and coercion. Temples were destroyed and local leaders pressured into baptism. Conversion was not only religious; it was a strategy to bind regions to royal authority.
next: Why did his rule collapse so quickly?

title: Foreign pressure and Danish dominance
content: After Olaf's death at Svolder, Norwegian politics fell under strong Danish influence. Cnut's North Sea Empire integrated Norway into wider Scandinavian and English power networks. This brought trade benefits but reduced autonomy.
next: Who resumed the project of Christian kingship inside Norway?

title: Olaf Haraldsson builds church and crown
content: Olaf Haraldsson sought tighter royal control while expanding church organization. He imported clergy, issued Christian law, and challenged local elites. His methods were often violent, creating enemies as quickly as institutions.
next: How did a defeated king become a national symbol?

title: Stiklestad and the cult of St. Olaf
content: Olaf died at Stiklestad in 1030, but reports of miracles around his burial transformed his legacy. As St. Olaf, he became Norway's patron saint. His cult gave Christian Norway a unifying symbol and strengthened royal legitimacy.

title: From Viking warfare to medieval governance
content: After 1066, Norway shifted away from overseas raiding toward domestic consolidation. Kings like Olaf the Quiet emphasized law, church building, and trade. Norway looked more like a medieval European monarchy and less like a Viking war confederation.
next: Why did civil war still return?

title: Civil wars and competing legitimacy
content: For over a century, factions fought over succession rules and elite alliances. Geography, weak institutions, and competing church-noble interests prolonged conflict. Yet these wars also pushed Norway toward clearer state structures.

title: Sverre and stronger monarchy
content: Sverre rose from contested origins to lead the Birkebeiner and defeat rivals. He challenged church political power while centralizing rule. His reign did not end all conflict, but it strengthened the crown's governing capacity.
next: When did medieval Norway reach its peak?

title: High medieval peak under Hakon Hakonsson
content: Hakon's reign brought comparative stability, court culture, and expanded influence across the North Atlantic. Iceland and Greenland accepted Norwegian overlordship, and Bergen grew as a political-commercial center. Norway briefly looked like a regional great power.
next: What institutional reforms made this power durable?

title: A unified law kingdom
content: Magnus VI issued a national law code that replaced many regional legal systems. This improved administrative coherence and reduced legal fragmentation. Law became a key tool of monarchy, not just local custom.
next: Why did this strong kingdom still move toward decline by 1319?

title: Dynastic break and vulnerability
content: When the male royal line ended, Norway entered personal union dynamics with neighbors. Small population, difficult geography, and external competition made sustained independence harder. The medieval high point had passed, and a more fragile era began.
next: Next: plague, unions, and centuries under Danish dominance.
